title: Characterizing force-chain network architecture in granular materials
speaker: Danielle Bassett
abstract: Force chains form heterogeneous physical structures that can constrain the mechanical stability and acoustic transmission of granular media. However, despite their relevance for predicting bulk properties of materials, there is no agreement on a quantitative description of force chains. Consequently, it is difficult to compare the force-chain structures in different materials or experimental conditions and to quantify their impact on materials properties.

proof_stages.label: Granular force networks as weighted graphs
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up photoelastic disk packings whose contact and force data are encoded as a network with particles as nodes, contacts as adjacency entries, forces as edge weights, and mean force as a baseline quantity.

proof_stages.label: Multiscale diagnostics from standard network statistics
proof_stages.summary: Off-the-shelf graph statistics—global efficiency, modularity, geodesic betweenness, and clustering coefficient—are used to detect system-wide, two-dimensional, one-dimensional, and local particle-scale structures in the force network.

proof_stages.label: Network predictors of acoustic transmission
proof_stages.summary: Experiments injecting an acoustic signal through a packing show that different network structures correlate with different phases of transmission, with efficiency capturing aspects of injection and modular domains capturing scattering/local trapping.

proof_stages.label: Contact-preserving null model for force-chain extraction
proof_stages.summary: The Newman-Girvan modularity null model is criticized as physically nonlocal for granular contacts, and it is replaced by a geographic null model proportional to the contact network, which extracts chain-like force structures rather than artificial two-dimensional domains.

proof_stages.label: Resolution selection by a branch-sensitive gap factor
proof_stages.summary: A modularity resolution parameter is introduced, and a gap factor based on the mismatch between topological hop distance and physical distance within communities is defined to choose the scale at which force chains are most branch-like.

proof_stages.label: Pressure and packing-type signatures in force-chain statistics
proof_stages.summary: The gap factor and related chain-level statistics change with applied pressure, distinguish laboratory frictional packings from frictionless simulations, and suggest diagnostic routes toward identifying mechanically stable or unstable regions.

proof_stages.label: Homological branch-stability statistic
proof_stages.summary: A future-direction statistic is proposed by forming clique complexes of extracted force chains, quotienting leaves to detect edge branches, and normalizing Betti-one so that loop-rich unstable branching can be separated from triangle-rich stable structure and shown to stratify pressure.

proof_stages.label: Dynamic force-chain tracking via zigzag homology
proof_stages.summary: For evolving packings, multilayer community detection is proposed to link force chains across parameter or time snapshots, after which refinements and induced homology maps can track which cycles persist or die using zigzag-homology ideas.

examples.name: Ring-lattice toy packing
examples.note: A one-dimensional ring/chain of consecutively contacting particles makes the Newman-Girvan null model's unphysical long-range connection probabilities visible, motivating the contact-preserving geographic null model.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly runs the null-model/modularity construction on the ring-lattice toy packing, comparing the Newman–Girvan matrix with a contact-preserving null model and deriving domain versus chain structures.

examples.name: Line, blob, and branch toy shapes
examples.note: The toy line, compact blob, and branched object show that hop distance and physical distance agree for non-branchy shapes but diverge for branches, leading directly to the gap-factor criterion.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes the line, blob, and branch shapes by computing hop distances versus physical distances in each and uses that comparison to motivate the gap factor.

examples.name: Stable triangle versus loose branch cartoon
examples.note: The cartoon contrast between densely triangulated branch points and loose edge branches motivates treating cliques as filled-in stable regions and remaining branches as homological loops.
examples.engagement_reason: The cartoon is reasoned through concretely: loose branches and stable triangles are compared mechanically, then interpreted as loops and cliques for a clique-complex/homology statistic.

passes.reason: the filled triangular clique is maximally locally connected and symmetric, contrasted with loose branches to motivate the homological statistic
